justification: All 6 required rules identified (BR-1 through BR-6) plus a bonus BR-7 (note immutability after locking). Regulatory rules (license verification BR-1, record visibility BR-5, locked note immutability BR-7) correctly marked invariant: true. The no-double-booking invariant (BR-2) is correctly invariant: true. Role-based cancellation correctly split into BR-3 (patient, invariant: false for the time threshold) and BR-4 (admin-only after window, invariant: true for the access control boundary). 12 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria provided, well exceeding the 4 minimum. Confidence level 72 is within the 65-90 range, with clearly stated assumptions about license verification mechanism, treating physician scope, and boundary conditions.

observations.text: Exceeded rule count with 7 rules, correctly splitting the consultation note locking (BR-6, time threshold as policy) from note immutability once locked (BR-7, invariant). This is a sophisticated distinction.

Invariant classifications are nuanced and well-justified. BR-3 (2-hour cancellation window) correctly marked invariant: false because the threshold is policy, while BR-4 (role escalation requirement) correctly marked invariant: true because the authorization model is a system integrity constraint.
12 acceptance criteria with excellent coverage including edge-adjacent scenarios like adjacent non-overlapping appointments (AC-4) and boundary time cases (AC-10/AC-11).
Rich edge cases section identifies critical scenarios like license revocation after booking, race conditions on concurrent bookings, and timezone/clock skew — showing deep domain understanding.
Escalations are well-structured with concrete options and recommendations, particularly the treating physician scope ambiguity and the addenda vs immutability distinction.
Confidence level of 72 appropriately reflects the well-defined core rules while acknowledging unspecified boundary conditions and missing lifecycle scenarios.

Minor: the bounded context is presented as a single combined context ('Appointment Scheduling & Clinical Records') rather than identifying them as two separate bounded contexts as the rubric expected. However, the domain analysis thoroughly covers both areas.
